coredata.dc:title: Mitochondria-targeted and pH-triggered charge-convertible polymeric micelles for anticancer therapy

Xu, Yanwen
Chen, Fengjiao
Yu, Dingle
Liang, Hongze

Liu, Ziyu

Liu, Junying
Tan, Hui
Zhao, Lingling
coredata.dc:description: Mitochondria-targeted and pH-triggered charge-convertible polymeric micelles (GPTD) were developed for doxorubicin (DOX) delivery and targeted anticancer therapy. GPTD surface was negatively charged under physiological pH (7.4), while it converted to positive charge under tumor pH (6.8). Sustained release of DOX from the drug loaded polymeric micelles was observed and the drug release was accelerated by decreasing the medium pH from 7.4 to 6.8. Cellular uptake by U251 glioma cells showed that GPTD can enhance the accumulation of polymeric micelles inside the mitochondria after charge-mediated cellular internalization and release DOX into the cellular interior. In vivo anticancer activity performed on glioma Balb/c nude mice demonstrated that the DOX-containing GPTD could remarkably inhibit the tumor growth, and displayed a significant reduction of tumor tissue cells and fewer Ki67 positive cells compared to the PBS group. Hence, this mitochondrial targeted and pH-triggered charge-convertible polymeric micellar system showed substantial promise for anticancer therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mitochondria-targeted
pH-triggered charge-convertible
Polymeric micelles
Drug delivery
Anticancer therapy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of DA and SA modified conjugates
Synthesis of fluorescence dye (FITC and Cy5) modified conjugates
Preparation of empty and DOX-containing polymeric micelles
Synthesis and characterization of GP, GPT and GPTD

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FTIR spectra of GC, GP, GPT and GPTD (A). The 1H NMR spectra of GP (B), GPT (C) and GPTD (D).

TEM images of GPTD (A) and GPTDD (B), variation of particle size with time (C), the zeta potentials of GPT, GPTD and GPTS at pH 7.4 and 6.8 (D), and the drug release curve of DOX-containing polymeric micelles in PBS at pH 7.4 and 6.8 (E).
Cell viability of U251 glioma cells assessed by CCK-8 assay after incubating with DOX-free (A), and DOX-containing micelles (B) at different concentrations for 24 h.

(A) CLSM images of U251 cells incubated with GPDF and GPTDF at pH 6.8 for 30 min. Mito-Tracker Red was applied to label mitochondria. (B) CLSM images of U251 cells incubated with DOX, GPDD and GPTDD for 24 h. Scale bars correspond to 50 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Biodistribution detection of DOX-free, and DOX-containing GPD and GPTD in glioma-bearing nude mice. Subcutaneous glioma model of Balb/c nude mice was made with U251 cell line. (A) In vivo fluorescent image of mice using IVIS Spectrum system 24 h after intravenously injecting Cy5-labeled GPD and GPTD. (B) Ex vivo fluorescence images of the main organs and tumors isolated from the glioma-bearing nude mice 24 h after injecting Cy5-labeled GPD and GPTD. (C) Qualitative biodistribution measurement of DOX in different organs and tumors of the mice 24 h after treating with GPDD and GPTDD.

Subcutaneous glioma model of Balb/c was used to observe the function of the polymeric micelles. Tumor size (A) and bodyweight (B) of glioma-bearing mice after treating with different polymeric micelles. *p < 0.05. (C) H&E and Ki-67 immunohistochemical staining on the glioma sections from subcutaneous model at 45 days after treating with PBS and polymeric micelles. The injection dose was 20 mg/kg for DOX of each drug. Scale bars correspond to 50 μm.
Illustration of the synthesis of GP, GPT, GPTD and GPTS (A), the self-assembly of polymeric micelles and pH triggered charge-conversion (B).

head.ce:title: Mitochondria-targeted and pH-triggered charge-convertible polymeric micelles for anticancer therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Endocrinology, The First Affiliated Hospital, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518035, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Endocrinology, The First Affiliated Hospital, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518035, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mitochondria-targeted and pH-triggered charge-convertible polymeric micelles (GPTD) were developed for doxorubicin (DOX) delivery and targeted anticancer therapy. GPTD surface was negatively charged under physiological pH (7.4), while it converted to positive charge under tumor pH (6.8). Sustained release of DOX from the drug loaded polymeric micelles was observed and the drug release was accelerated by decreasing the medium pH from 7.4 to 6.8. Cellular uptake by U251 glioma cells showed that GPTD can enhance the accumulation of polymeric micelles inside the mitochondria after charge-mediated cellular internalization and release DOX into the cellular interior. In vivo anticancer activity performed on glioma Balb/c nude mice demonstrated that the DOX-containing GPTD could remarkably inhibit the tumor growth, and displayed a significant reduction of tumor tissue cells and fewer Ki67 positive cells compared to the PBS group. Hence, this mitochondrial targeted and pH-triggered charge-convertible polymeric micellar system showed substantial promise for anticancer therapy.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Malignant tumors have become one of the most serious diseases worldwide, causing millions of deaths every year and posing a great threat to human health [1]. At present, chemotherapy, surgery, and radiotherapy are still the main methods of tumor treatment in clinic [2,3]. However, the chemotherapeutic efficacy of tumor treatment is far from ideal because chemotherapeutic drugs are toxic to normal tissues and have serious side effects due to the non-targeting function [4,5]. Furthermore, chemotherapeutic drugs frequently encounter multidrug resistance (MDR), which causes poor clinical efficacy or even therapeutic failure of many chemotherapeutic drugs. MDR generally arises from stress response proteins, hinting at apoptotic pathways as well as decreasing drug uptake due to the overexpression of drug efflux pumps [6,7]. Various strategies have been developed to improve traditional chemotherapy for accurate drug delivery to lesion site using drug delivery systems based on nanotechnology [8]. In recent years, mitochondrial-targeted nanocarriers have become a notable strategy for overcoming MDR in cancer chemotherapy [9–11]. Mitochondria are DNA containing intracellular organelles [12,13], which play an important role in cellular function including energy generation (i.e., Adenosine Triphosphate, ATP), cell survival, cell apoptosis, ROS (Reactive Oxygen Species) stabilization, and so on [14,15]. Triphenylphosphonium, a mitochondrial targeting moiety, is frequently used to decorate the surface of nanocarriers for mitochondrial delivery because it can be uptake by mitochondrial membrane due to its high lipophilicity and cationic charge [16,17].

It is reported that surface charge of nanocarriers is a key factor for the enhancement of tumor accumulation and cellular uptake [18]. Nanoparticles with positive surface charge can interact with negatively charged cancer cell membranes and thus facilitate internalization [19]. However, permanent positively charged nanoparticles are toxic to red blood cells and are likely to be cleared during blood circulation [19]. Whereas negatively charged nanoparticles can prolong blood circulation by inhibiting protein adsorption originated from electrostatic repulsion and enhance tumor targeting through the enhanced permeation and retention (EPR) effect [20,21]. This problem can be solved by the development of surface charge-convertible nanocarriers, which have negatively charged surfaces in blood circulation and change to positive surface charge at tumor site [22,23]. As is known to all, the tumor extracellular environment has a pH value ranged from 6.5 to 6.8, which is a bit lower than that of normal tissues (7.4) [24]. Thus, considering the pH differences among tumor sites and normal tissue, pH-triggered charge-convertible nanocarriers were widely studied for tumor targeting delivery and enhance the stability of nanoparticles through charge shielding [25]. To date, various pH-sensitive groups have been introduced to design pH-triggered charge-convertible nanoparticles, such as acid-labile amide bonds, benzoic imine bonds, zwitterionic groups, and amine groups [18,19].

To this end, we aimed to develop tumor pH-triggered charge convertible polymeric micelles for mitochondria-targeted anticancer drug delivery to enhance the chemotherapeutic effect. Poly (lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) was conjugated to glycol chitosan (GC) to obtain an amphiphilic polymer conjugate GC-PLGA. Then, (4-Carboxybutyl) triphenylphosphonium bromide (TPP) was decorated in GC-PLGA as a mitochondrial targeting ligand, and 2, 3-dimethylmaleic anhydride (DA) was conjugated to GC-PLGA as a charge-convertible moiety through dimethyl maleic amides, which convert cationic charges (amino groups) to net negative charges (carboxyl groups). Dimethyl maleic amides show pH-dependent hydrolysis, that is stable in the physiological environment (pH 7.4) and can be hydrolyzed quickly when pH < 7.0 to recover the amine groups [26,27]. DOX, a broad-spectrum anticancer drug that interacts with DNA by inhibiting the biosynthesis of macromolecular, was selected as a model drug. For the obtained multifunctional polymeric micelles, the physiochemical characteristics, drug release behavior, cytotoxicity and cellular uptake were investigated. Moreover, the biodistribution and therapeutic effects were evaluated in vivo. As expected, the polymeric micellar system exhibited an improved anticancer efficacy, suggesting a promising platform for chemotherapy.

GC (purity (titation) ≥ 60 %, Mw ≥ 82000), sigma; PLGA (lactide:glycolide 75:25; MW ∼ 9500), Dalian Meilun Biotechnology Co., ltd.; N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), Quzhou Xinteng Chemical Co., ltd.; DA, TPP, maleic anhydride (SA), morpholine ethanesulfonic acid (MES) and Cyanine 5 monosuccinimidyl ester (Cy5), Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., ltd.; DOX and Fluorescein isothiocyanate isomer (FITC), Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., ltd.; 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC·HCl), Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd.

The synthesis of GP was referred to the method reported in previous work [28]. Briefly, PLGA (0.2 g), EDC·HCl (14.4 mg), and NHS (10.8 mg) dissolved in DMSO (24 mL) were stirred at 25 °C for 2 h to activate the carboxyl group of PLGA. Then 6 mL of GC solution (0.2 g dissolved in deionized water) was added dropwise. After 12 h, the mixture was dialyzed against deionized water for three days using a dialyzed tubing (MWCO, 8000–14,000 Da) with water change every 4 h and then lyophilized to harvest a white cotton-like product at a yield of ∼ 80 %.

TPP was anchored to GC according to the previous report [26]. In brief, 166.5 mg of GP was dissolved in 45 mL of mixed solvent (0.1 mM MES and DMSO with a volume ratio of 1:1). Then, 35.5 mg of TPP, 76.68 mg of EDC·HCl, and 31.76 mg of NHS were added. The mixture was stirring at 25 °C for 24 h, and then dialyzed against deionized water for three days using a dialyzed tubing (MWCO, 8000–14000 Da) with water change every 4 h. The residual was lyophilized to harvest a white cotton-like product at a yield of ∼ 76.4 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of DA and SA modified conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: 61 mg of GPT was dissolved in 10 mL of mixed solvent (0.1 mM MES and DMSO with a volume ratio of 1:1) at pH 8.5 (adjusted by 1 M HCl). Then, 4 mL of DA solution (95 mg dissolved in DMSO) was added dropwise at pH 8–8.5. After stirring at 25 °C for 3 h, the mixture was dialyzed against deionized water for three days using a dialyzed tubing (MWCO, 8000–14000 Da) with water change every 4 h and then freeze-dried to harvest a white cotton-like product at a yield of ∼ 83.2 %. The synthesized sample was called GPTD.
DA modified GP (GPD) and SA modified GPT (GPTS) were synthesized as described above using different reagents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescence dye (FITC and Cy5) modified conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: 200 µL of FITC solution (0.46 mg dissolved in methanol) was added dropwise to 3 mL of GP solution (50 mg dissolved in deionized water) under stirring at 25 °C. The mixture was dialyzed against deionized water for three days using a dialyzed tubing (MWCO, 8000–14000 Da) with water change every 4 h, and then lyophilized to harvest a yellow cotton-like product at a yield of ∼ 89.4 %.
The FITC labeled GPT, GPD and GPTD were synthesized as described above. FITC labeled GP, GPT, GPD and GPTD was called GPF, GPTF, GPDF and GPTDF.
The Cy5 labeled GPD and GPTD were prepared by the same protocol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of empty and DOX-containing polymeric micelles
ce:section.ce:para: The formation of polymeric micelles was prepared by a dialysis method according to previous work [30]. Briefly, the synthesized polymer conjugate was dissolved in 5 mL of DMSO at 10 mg mL−1 and then added into 10 mL of methanol under stirring. After stirring for 2 h at 25 °C, 10 mL PBS was added dropwise. The mixture was stirred for another 24 h at 25 °C, and then dialyzed against deionized water for three days. The residual was lyophilized and a white powder-like product was harvest.

For DOX-containing polymeric micelles, 100 μL of triethylamine was added dropwise into the DOX solution (50 mg dissolved in 10 mL of methanol) for deacidification. The mixture was stirred at 25 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the polymeric conjugate dissolved in DMSO was added and stirred for another 2 h. Subsequent steps were the same as described above. After dialysis and lyophilization, a red powder-like product was obtained. The amount of loaded DOX was measured by fluorescence spectrometry at 598 nm using a DOX calibration curve in DMSO. DOX loaded GP, GPT, GPD and GPTD were called GPD, GPTD, GPDD and GPTDD, respectively.

FTIR was performed using a Nicolet 6700 (Nicolet, USA) spectrometer. 1H NMR was performed on a Bruker Fourier 400 MHz spectrometer in DMSO‑d 6. The zeta potential and particle size were determined by the Zeta sizer Nano-ZS90 instrument (Malvern, UK). The morphology of the polymeric micelles was observed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL-1011, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) after staining with 0.5 wt% phosphotungstic acids.

The DOX release behavior was investigated as previous work [29]. Briefly, 1 mL of the polymeric micelles dispersed in 0.01 M PBS (pH 7.4) at a concentration of 1 mg mL−1 was put into a dialysis tubing (MWCO, 8000–14000 Da). Then, the dispersion was placed in a brown vial with 10 mL of PBS containing 0.1 % (v/v) Tween-80 at 37 °C. At predetermined time point, 2 mL of PBS was taken out from the vial and then replenished with 2 mL of fresh medium. The amount of released DOX was tested using fluorescence spectroscopy at 594 nm excited at 494 nm. The results were presented as the average of three parallel tests with standard deviations.

CCK-8 assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of the polymeric micelles. Briefly, 100 μL of DMEM containing 1.5 × 103 U251 glioma cells were seeded in 96-well plates. After 24 h incubation, the culture medium was removed and replaced with 100 μL of DMEM containing polymeric micelles with or without DOX at various concentrations. The cells were incubated for another 24 h, and then the culture medium was replaced with 100 μL of DMEM containing CCK-8 assay (10 µL). After incubation for another 4 h, the absorbance of each well was read under a microplate reader (Thermo MULTISKAN MK3) at 450 nm. Each sample was tested 5 times.

U251 glioma cells (1 × 105 cells/well) were seeded on 24-well plates and incubated for 24 h. Then, DOX, GPDD, and GPTDD dispersed in DMEM (4 µg mL−1 of DOX) was added and incubated at 37 °C for another 24 h. The cells were treated with trypsin after discarding the culture medium. Subsequently, cells were collected by centrifugation and re-suspended in PBS. The fluorescence intensity of the cell suspension was recorded at 520 nm (excitated at 488 nm) using a BD Accuri™ C6 Plus flow cytometer.

Intracellular localization of GPDF and GPTDF. U251 glioma cells were seeded on 12-well plates at 5 × 104 cells/well. Mito-Tracker Red CMXRos working solution (Beyotime, 40 nM) was applied to label the mitochondria. At the same time, GPDF and GPTDF dispersed in culture medium (pH 7.4 or 6.8, 100 µg/mL) was added to the cells, and then incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, cells were washed with PBS and incubated in 4 % paraformaldehyde for 30 min before the cells were observed with CLSM (Zeiss, German). FITC was excited at 488 nm with the emission at 520 nm and Mito-Tracker Red CMXRos was excited at 560 nm with the emission at 590 nm.

Intracellular DOX release. U251 glioma cells and C8 astrocyte cells were seeded on 24-well plates at 1 × 105 cells/well, respectively. After 24 h incubation at 37 °C, DOX, GPDD and GPTDD dispersed in DMEM (4 µg mL−1 of DOX) were added. The cells were incubation for another 24 h, and then rinsed with PBS three times. U251 cells and C8 astrocyte cells were subsequently observed using CLSM (Zeiss, German) at 550 nm (excited at 488 nm), respectively.

The animal experiments were conducted following the ethical guidelines approved by the Ethics Committee of Peking University Shenzhen Graduate School. Balb/c 7 nude mice with 4 to 5 weeks old were used. 0.2 mL of sterile PBS containing ∼ 1 × 106 U251 glioma cells was inoculated into the subcutaneous fashions of mice. The tumor size and bodyweight of the nude mice in each group were recorded using a caliper and electronic balance, respectively. The tumor size of nude mice was calculated by this formula:
a ×
b
2
/ 2 ,
where a and b was the length and width of the tumor, respectively. The tumor-bearing nude mice were randomly divided into several groups when the tumor size reached ∼ 100 mm3, with 5–6 mice in each group for in vivo tests described below.

The tumor-bearing nude mice were intravenously treated by Cy5-labeled GPD and GPTD. 24 h later, images were recorded with IVIS Spectrum system (Excitation: 470 nm, Emission 590 nm). Subsequently, the mice were sacrificed and the major organs were excised for ex vivo imaging.

For the quantitative drug distribution experiments, tumor-bearing nude mice were intravenously treated by GPDD and GPTDD. After 24 h, the organs were isolated and homogenized in 70 % ethanol with 3 N HCl. The supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 13,000 rpm for 10 min, and the amount of DOX was determined by fluorescence (Excitation: 470 nm, Emission 590 nm).

The glioma-bearing mice were randomly divided into six groups (n = 6). After administrating with PBS, GPTD, GPTD, GPDD, GPTDD, and GPTSD, respectively, the tumor size and bodyweight of mice in each group were recorded every 2 days. After treatment 45 days, the nude mice were sacrificed and the tumors were excised for staining H&E and Ki-67.
One-way ANOVA method was used to assess the statistical significance by SPSS software 25.0. If the p value was lower than 0.05, it was considered to have significant differences.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GP, GPT and GPTD
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of GPTD was illustrated in Scheme 1 A. Fig. 1 A shows the FTIR spectrum of GC, GP, GPT, and GPTD. As for GC, the band between 3450 and 3500 cm−1 was corresponding to OH stretching in GC, and the band at 1631 cm−1 was corresponding to CO stretching of amide I in the residual N-acetyl group in GC [31]. In the FITR spectrum of GP, the band at 1759 cm−1 was corresponding to the CO bond stretching of the esters in PLGA, and the bands at 1092 and 1186 cm−1 were attributed to the CO stretching of aliphatic polyesters [31]. The degree of substitution (DS) of GC with PLGA is 2.4 % calculated based on the element analysis (Table S1) by comparing the carbon/nitrogen (C/N) molar ratio. For GPT, the band at 1450 cm−1 was corresponding to Ph-P stretching in TPP [9]. And for GPTD, the presence of band at 1706 cm−1 was attributed to CO stretching of the carboxylate group derived from DA [30].

The chemical structure of GP, GPT and GPTD was further confirmed by 1H NMR. In Fig. 1B, the peaks at δ 5.05 and 1.31 were assigned to the protons of CH and CH3 in lactide moiety, respectively. The peak at δ 4.73 was assigned to the methyl group in glycolide [32]. And the broad peak between δ 2.90 and 3.62 in GP were corresponding to the protons of the GC unit. The DS of GC with PLGA was 2.0 % calculated based on the 1H NMR spectrum of GP by enlarging and integrating of the diagnostic signals. The result was close to the value (2.4 %) determined using elemental analysis by comparing the C/N molar ratio. For GPT, the presence of peaks between δ 7.75 and 7.91 was attributed to the phenyl ring of TPP, and signals at δ 1.84 and 1.47 were attributed to the CH2 proton of the carboxy butyl linker (Fig. 1C) [33]. The calculated DS of GC with PLGA and TPP was 0.8 % and 2.2 % respectively by enlarging and integrating of the diagnostic signals based on the 1H NMR spectrum of GPT. And for GPTD, the peak at δ 1.88 was assigned to the methyl group in DA (Fig. 1D) [34]. This peak was overlapped with the peak at δ 1.84 attributed to the methylene group in TPP, thus resulted in the enhancement of the peak when compared with GPT. The calculated DS of GC with PLGA, TPP and DA was 0.7 %, 2.5 % and 5.8 % respectively by enlarging and integrating of the diagnostic signals based on the 1H NMR spectrum of GPTD. The graft amount of PLGA in GPT and GPTD was less than that in GP, and this may be caused by the partial hydrolysis of PLGA from the GC backbone during the subsequent synthetic process. All these results confirmed the successful synthesis of GP, GPT and GPTD.

GPTD could self-assemble to form polymeric micelles in solution. The CMC of GPTD was 0.010 mg mL−1 determined by I372/I383 of pyrene (Figure S1). The hydrodynamic size of GPTD was 242.0 ± 4.0 nm determined by DLS, and TEM images showed spherical morphology of the polymeric micelles (Fig. 2 A). DOX was selected as a model drug to assess the in vitro and in vivo properties of the polymeric micelles. The morphology of GPTDD maintained a spherical shape as GPTD with a darker domain in the nucleus of micelles due to DOX loading (Fig. 2B), while the DLS size of GPTDD increased after DOX loading. The drug loading content (DLC) of GPTDD was 31.96 %, and the drug loading efficiency (DLE) was 56.52 %, determined by fluorescence spectrometry at 598 nm (Table S2). The relative stability of the polymeric micelles was evaluated by incubating them in PBS for 82 h, and the sizes were measured by DLS at intervals. As shown in Fig. 2C, the variation of particle size showed similar tendency. The particle size had a slight increase within the first 10 h, followed a slight decrease and then a slight increase again during the next 72 h. The increase and decrease of particle size may be due to the polymer swelling and release of DOX from the micelles in PBS during the incubation time, respectively.

Fig. 2D showed that the zeta potential of GPT demonstrated positive charges at both pH 7.4 (2.0 ± 2.7 mV) and 6.8 (46.8 ± 12.9 mV) due to the amino groups in GC. While GPD and GPTD exhibited negative charges at pH 7.4 (-15.2 ± 0.8 and −13.3 ± 2.5 mV) and conversed to positive charges when decreasing pH to 6.8 (4.6 ± 2.0 and 22.6 ± 5.9 mV). This may be due to the pH-dependent hydrolysis characteristic of dimethyl maleic amide, which is stable at pH 7.4 and can break the amide bond quickly in mildly acidic environments (pH < 7.0) [26]. To further study the surface charge conversion of GPTD induced by acidic pH, GPTS formed from GPT and SA was used as a reference. As demonstrated in Fig. 2D, GPTS exhibited negative charges of −50.2 ± 3.1 and −35.2 ± 3.6 mV at pH 7.4 and 6.8, respectively. This is because the amide bonds formed from amine groups and succinic anhydrides in GPTS are stable at both pH 7.4 and lower pH values [35]. This pH-triggered surface charge conversion characteristic of GPTD is not only beneficial to the long-term circulation of particles in blood (physiological pH), but also can improve the cellular uptake efficiency of particles after accumulation in tumour (mildly acidic pH) [36].

Sustained release of DOX without initial burst from GPTD and GPTDD was observed, and the DOX release was accelerated by lowering the medium pH from 7.4 (physiological pH) to 6.8 (tumor acidic pH) (Fig. 2E). The cumulative release of DOX from GPTD is 42.3 and 47.6 % at 130 h under pH 7.4 and 6.8, respectively. The accelerated drug release at acidic pH may be contributed by the swelling of polymeric micelles which resulted from the increased electrostatic repulsion caused by the amino protonation in GPTD [30]. As for GPTDD, the DOX release was slower than that from GPTD, while the drug release accelerated at acidic pH was more obvious compared with GPTD. As shown, the cumulative release of DOX from GPTDD is 33.6 and 43.2 % at a release time of 143 h under pH 7.4 and 6.8, respectively. The accelerated drug release at acidic pH may be resulted from the swelling of polymeric micelles and the cleavage of DA. Generally, the Ritger-Peppas equation (Mt /M0 = ktn ) is applied to describe the drug release behavior. Mt /M0 is the drug released fraction at time t, while n is the diffusion-related component [37]. The diffusion component n, which depends on the polymer swelling characteristics, can be calculated based on Fig. 2E using Origin software. The obtained n values are 0.80, 0.74, 0.86 and 0.74 for GPTDD at pH 6.8, GPTDD at pH 7.4, GPTD at pH 7.4 and GPTD at pH 6.8, respectively, which indicates a non-Fickian (anomalous) diffusion mechanism for the drug release, meaning that the diffusion of drug molecules is through both the swollen and hydrolysis of the polymer matrix.

Fig. 3 A demonstrated that all the empty polymeric micelles did not lead to a further decrease in U251 cell viability with increasing concentration. Negatively charged GPTD exhibited lower cytotoxicity than that of GPT with positively charged surface between 30 and 1000 μg mL−1. The cytotoxicity of DOX-containing micelles against U251 cells was increased with the increasing concentration. GPDD and GPTSD inhibited cell viability to approximately less than 40 % at 20 μg mL−1 (Fig. 3B). Furthermore, the toxicity of DOX-containing polymeric micelles was higher than that of empty polymeric micelles, while it was lower than free DOX at the corresponding DOX concentration because of the encapsulation and sustained release of drug.

In order to investigate the targeting efficiency of the polymeric micelles to mitochondria, FITC labeled GPD and GPTD (GPDF and GPTDF) were incubated with U251 cells. As shown in Fig. 4 A, FITC showed green fluorescence and the mitochondria of U251 cells were stained in red by Mito-Tracker. For GPDF, there is only a small overlapped part between green and red fluorescence. In contrast, the FITC and Mito-Tracker of GPTDF group overlapped very well, and GPTDF was not distributed in cells except in mitochondria, indicating the intensive accumulations of GPTD in mitochondria due to the high affinity of TPP to mitochondria, which confirms the specifical accumulation of polymeric micelles in the mitochondrial region after TPP modification.

To verify the cellular uptake and DOX release inside the cells, DOX-containing polymeric micelles were incubated with U251 and C8 cells respectively, and CLSM was used to observe the accumulation inside the cells. CLSM images showed strong fluorescence intensity of DOX in the U251 cells treated with DOX, GPDD and GPTDD for 24 h (Fig. 4B). The results showed red fluorescence mainly originated from the nuclear of U251 cells incubated with free DOX, indicating crucial internalization of DOX molecules by cell nuclei. While red fluorescence mainly originated from the cytoplasm and partly from the nucleus of U251 cells incubated with GPDD, probably due to the internalization of DOX-containing micelles via endocytosis [29]. More DOX was found in the nucleus and less in the cytoplasm of GPTDD-incubated cells, indicating DOX release in the nucleus, which is positive for the anticancer therapy because DOX was intercalated with DNA [30]. It was noted that the results were different from that observed in normal cells C8. As shown in Figure S3, red fluorescence can be observed in C8 cells incubated with free DOX, while little red fluorescence was observed in C8 cells incubated with GPDD and GPTDD. This may be due to the different surface charges of polymer micelles under different incubation conditions. As reported, the pH value is 7.4 and 6.5–6.8 in normal tissue and tumor tissue, respectively. Thus, for C8 normal cells, the negatively surface-charged is adverse to the cellular uptake of polymeric micelles, so little red fluorescence was observed in C8 cells incubated with GPDD and GPTDD. And for U251 glioma cells, the surface charge of polymeric micelles conversed to positive under weak acid condition, which facilitates the cellular uptake of polymeric micelles and intracellular DOX release. Alternatively, the accumulation of DOX in U251 cells was examined using FACS. The results displayed that the fluorescence intensity of U251 treated with GPDD and GPTDD was stronger than the negative control (Figure S4), suggesting the effective cellular uptake of DOX-containing micelles on glioma cells.

To explore the biodistribution of GPD and GPTD micelles in glioma-bearing mice, in vivo fluorescence images were obtained in subcutaneous glioma model of Balb/c nude mice at 24 h after treating with Cy5-labeled GPTD and GPD. Fig. 5 A showed retention of Cy5-GPTD and Cy5-GPD in the position of tumor and the upper part of tail 24 h following injection. Moreover, Cy5-GPTD was found in ex vivo tumor, lung, kidney, liver, and spleen, and Cy5-GPD existed in ex vivo tumor, lung, kidney, and liver (Fig. 5B). We further determined the quantity of DOX in ex vivo heart, tumor, lung, kidney, liver, and spleen after treating with GPDD and GPTDD for 24 h. Results demonstrated DOX from GPDD and GPTDD could be released in tumor tissues (Fig. 5C). Although both DOX and polymeric micelles were found in tumor and other organs, there's much more DOX in liver and spleen. This could be attributed to the nanoparticle uptake by the macrophages in the reticuloendothelial system [38]. The results showed a similar trend with the biodistribution of nanoparticles reported in the literatures [38–39].

To evaluate the potential therapeutic efficacy of the polymeric micellar system, tumor growth trend after treatment was investigated in subcutaneous glioma models. Fig. 6 A showed the mean relative value of tumor volumes in each group during the treatment. As shown, the tumor in the PBS group grew very fast, and the growth of tumors on empty polymeric micelles GPTD was slightly inhibited in the first 10 days, probably due to the antibacterial activity of chitosan [40], while the tumor grew again subsequently. The growth of tumor was remarkably inhibited for 22 days after the administration of GPTD (p < 0.05), GPDD (p < 0.05) and GPTSD (p < 0.05) compared to PBS treatment. It is noted that the GPTDD treated group showed significant inhibition of tumor growth during the investigation period compared with other groups (p < 0.05), possibly due to the mitochondrial targeting and positively charged surface enhanced cellular uptake. In addition, the bodyweight of nude mice in each group was not obviously affected during the experimental period (Fig. 6B), suggesting minor side effects of the polymeric micelles to animals.

H&E staining was performed on glioma sections from GPDD and GPTDD treated mice to observe the growth of tumor cells. The results showed higher level of tumor cells damage in DOX-loaded polymeric micelles treated glioma sections than the PBS group, and GPTDD group demonstrated more vacancies and a remarkable reduction of tumor tissue cells than that of GPDD group (Fig. 6C), indicating good consistency with the tumor growth data. In addition, Ki67 antigen staining was also performed to investigate the antitumor efficacy on tumor cell proliferation. As shown in Fig. 6C, the GPDD and GPTDD groups resulted in fewer Ki67 positive cells compared to the PBS group, and the GPTDD group showed fewer Ki67 positive cells compared to the GPDD group, suggesting that the U251 cell proliferation in GPTDD treated group was much less active, and the antitumor activity was considerable higher compared to other groups. The results were in good consistency with the tumor growth data and H&E staining.

Chemotherapeutic drugs frequently encounter problems such as the toxicity to normal tissues, serious side effects and MDR, which resulted in poor therapeutic efficacy. Recent studies have shown that mitochondrial targeting appears to be a worthwhile strategy for overcoming MDR in cancer chemotherapy as MDR induced by drug efflux pump overexpression requires the help of ATP [39]. Herein, in this work, TPP was used to modify the surface of polymeric micelles as a mitochondrial targeting ligand. FTIR and 1H NMR confirmed the successful modification of TPP on the polymeric micelles GPT and GPTD (Fig. 1). However, when TPP-modified polymeric micelles (GPTD) and polymeric micelles without TPP-modification (GPD) were co-incubated with U251 cells respectively under normal condition (physiological pH 7.4), there was no significant difference in the specific accumulation of them in mitochondria (Fig. S3). This may be related to the surface charge of the polymeric micelles. As shown in Fig. 2D, the zeta potential of GPD, and GPTD demonstrated negative charges at pH 7.4, which was not conductive to the uptake of polymeric micelles by U251 cells because the cellular membrane is also negatively charged.

It is well known that the pH value at tumor extracellular environment is ranging from 6.5 to 6.8, which is a bit lower than that of normal tissues (7.4) [24]. In this work, tumor pH-triggered charge-conversion strategy was applied in the polymeric micellar system. Therefore, when GPTD and GPD were co-incubated with U251 cells respectively under weak acidic condition (tumor pH 6.8), there was a specifical accumulation of GPTD in the mitochondria due to TPP modification as well as acid-triggered charge-conversion (Fig. 4A). In addition, polymeric micelles with negatively charged surfaces can prolong the circulation time in blood and thus enhance their accumulation at tumor site through the EPR effect. As shown in the results of in vivo biodistribution, retention of Cy5-labelled GPTD and GPD in the position of tumor could be found 24 h after injection, and the result was corresponded with that of ex vivo tumor imaging. After a long period of circulation in the body, a significant amount of the polymer micelles reached the tumor site, although they were also observed in other organs (Fig. 5A and 5B). Once the polymeric micelles reached the tumor site, the surface charge conversed from negative-charge to passive-charge by acid-triggering, which could facilitate the internalization of polymeric micelles by tumor cells and release the cargo inside the cells. This was confirmed by the intracellular and in vivo release of DOX. As demonstrated in Fig. 4B and 5C, accumulation of DOX in U251 cells and tumor tissues was observed. While the amount of DOX accumulation in tumor is low, and surface modification using tumor-targeting ligands (such as folate, RGD peptide and so on) could be considered to improve the tumor accumulation in the future work.

One of the important roles of nanocarriers is to continuously release drugs in a controlled manner and reduce the toxic and side effects of non-specific drugs. In the present work, DOX was encapsulated in the polymeric micelles. The polymeric micelles with negative-charged surfaces showed good biocompatibility (cell viability ≥ 80 %) as demonstrated by CCK-8 assay (Fig. 3A), and DOX could sustained release from the polymeric micellar nanocarriers without initial burst (Fig. 2E). Thus, the toxicity of polymeric micelles containing DOX was higher than that of empty polymeric micelles and lower than that of free DOX (Fig. 3B). Moreover, the DOX release can be accelerated by lowering the medium pH from 7.4 to 6.8. Considering the weak acidic microenvironment of tumor, the pH-triggered drug release of the polymeric micellar system is of great significance in the tumor chemotherapy. Based on the above factors, the mitochondria-targeted and pH-triggered charge-convertible polymeric micelles (GPTDD) showed a good in vivo anti-tumor efficacy in subcutaneous glioma models against Balb/c nude mice, demonstrating a significantly inhibition in tumor growth, a remarkable reduction of tumor tissue cells, and fewer Ki67 positive cells compared with other groups (Fig. 6). However, the absolute tumor inhibition effect of GPTDD was not so satisfying, and synergistic therapy by co-delivery of photothermal or photodynamic agents (such as ICG, black phosphorus and so on) could be considered to improve the tumor inhibition effect in the future work.

In this work, a novel polymeric micelle with mitochondria-targeted and pH-triggered charge-convertible capability was explored to enhance the efficacy of anticancer therapy and reduce side effect. DOX could be sustained release from the micelles and the drug release was accelerated by changing the medium pH from 7.4 to 6.8. CCK-8 assay showed that the empty polymeric micelles had good biocompatibility with low cytotoxicity on U251 glioma cells and the cytotoxicity was enhanced after DOX encapsulation. In vivo anticancer activity performed on glioma Balb/c nude mice showed that the DOX-loaded polymeric micelles could significantly inhibited the tumor growth. H&E and Ki67 staining demonstrated remarkable reduction of tumor tissue cells and less Ki67 positive cells in GPDD and GPTDD treated groups compared to PBS group. Thus, the mitochondrial targeted and pH-triggered charge-convertible polymeric micelles have promise potential in targeted anticancer therapy.
